description: Stunning hummingbird magnet with tubular orange flowers from spring through frost. Thrives in part shade — one of the few flowering shrubs that performs well under trees. Evergreen in Zones 9+; dies back in hard freezes but regrows reliably. Spreads slowly by suckers. Combines beautifully with salvias and turk's cap in a shade guild.

Native Texas perennial shrub with distinctive swirled scarlet flowers that never fully open. Blooms summer through fall — a critical nectar source for Ruby-throated Hummingbirds during fall migration. Thrives in shade where little else blooms. Dies back to roots in winter and regrows vigorously. Red fruits edible and attract mockingbirds.

Pale pink cultivar of Texas native Turk's Cap selected by Texas horticulturist Pam Puryear. Same robust shade tolerance and hummingbird appeal as the red species, with softer color for garden contrast. Slightly more compact habit. Excellent in combination with the red form for extended bloom and layered color.

Low-growing native groundcover with clusters of purple-pink flowers from February through November. Spreads by runners to form a weed-suppressing mat 12-18 inches high. Exceptional butterfly and bee plant — a major nectar source for monarchs during spring migration. Extremely drought-tolerant on rocky limestone soils. Self-seeds freely once established.

Texas endemic native growing naturally on limestone cliffs and canyon walls. Bright red tubular flowers bloom summer through fall — a peak hummingbird season plant when many others fade. Extremely heat and drought tolerant once established in well-drained soil. Evergreen in mild winters. One of the most spectacular native perennials for rocky Central Texas landscapes.

Texas native sage with long spikes of powdery blue flowers from spring through hard freeze. One of the best native plants for attracting bumblebees and cloudless sulphur butterflies. Extremely heat and drought tolerant. Self-seeds prolifically — a dependable annual in most of Texas, short-lived perennial in zones 8b+. Works as a durable groundcover-height insectary throughout the food forest.

The most popular cultivar of Texas native Autumn Sage — brilliant cherry-red flowers nearly year-round in mild winters. More floriferous and compact than the species. Rated one of the top hummingbird plants in Texas. Extremely drought-tolerant and heat-hardy. Deer resistant. Cut back hard in late winter for dense regrowth. Excellent front-of-border or groundcover companion in a food forest guild.

Showstopping Texas native shrub covered in trumpet-shaped yellow flowers summer through frost. Fast-growing (6-8ft in one season). Exceptional hummingbird and butterfly plant. Dies back in hard freezes but regrows powerfully from roots in spring. Tolerates poor, alkaline, rocky soils where almost nothing else thrives. One of the longest-blooming and most drought-tolerant shrubs for Texas food forests.

Texas's signature native grass — the backbone of the original tall grass and mixed grass prairies. Spectacular year-round interest: blue-green in summer, copper-orange-red in fall and winter. Fluffy white seed heads provide winter bird food. Clump-forming, non-spreading. Exceptional on dry rocky soils where other grasses fail. Provides habitat for ground-nesting bees. Outstanding pollinator insectary plant when in seed.
